text: Lactate threshold (the VO2 at which blood lactate begins to increase above resting level) is more strongly correlated with VO2max and endurance running performance than LT1, LT2, or OBLA markers.
Four common lactate parameters (LT, LT1, LT2, and OBLA) are all highly correlated with each other during incremental exercise testing.
All lactate-based thresholds (LT, LT1, LT2, OBLA) are significantly correlated with both VO2max and 12-minute running performance.
In untrained females, lactate threshold occurs at approximately 40% of VO2max, while OBLA occurs at approximately 84% of VO2max.
specific_value: LT at ~14.5 ml/kg/min (40% VO2max), OBLA at ~30.3 ml/kg/min (84% VO2max)

text: OBLA (onset of blood lactate accumulation) is defined as the VO2 at which blood lactate reaches 4 mM, while LT2 uses a fixed 2 mM threshold — these represent distinct physiological breakpoints useful for prescribing training intensity.
Lactate threshold is the best single index for predicting both aerobic capacity and endurance running performance among lactate-based markers.
